data.title: Stryker maintains 2026 outlook of 8%-9.5% organic sales growth and $14.90-$15.10 adjusted EPS amid cyber incident recovery
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Stryker (SYK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "The cyber incident had a big impact on our results" and "resulted in distortions in our first quarter results that will normalize over the course of the year," said (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo), adding, "this situation will not enable us to provide the normal level of detail or explanations that you are accustomed to hearing from us."

  • "As of the week of April 1, we were fully operational across our global manufacturing network as we continue to meet demand and support patient care," said (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo), after noting the incident "occurred late in the quarter and resulted in a global disruption to our business operations."

  • "For the first quarter, organic sales growth was 2.4%" with "1.9% in the U.S. and 3.9% internationally," said (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo), while stating, "we are maintaining our full year guidance" despite the cyber incident impact.

  • "We recently announced the agreement to acquire Amplitude Vascular Systems" and "expect to close in the second quarter," said (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo), adding that the acquisition "will help expand treatment options for our peripheral vascular customers" and is "a step toward expanding our presence in the broader cardiovascular space."

  • "At the beginning of Q1, we established our new Ortho Tech business" to "simplify the customer experience, accelerate innovation and increase our speed to market," said (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo).

  • "Finally, I'd like to thank Jason for his last 4 years as Vice President of Finance and Investor Relations" and "we look forward to Nick Mead assuming the role of VP and Investor Relations on May 1," said (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo).

  • "Our capital order book remains elevated" and "we delivered our best ever Q1 for Mako installations both in the U.S. and internationally," said (VP and Group CFO of MedSurg & Neurotechnology Jason Beach), adding the company expects "sustained momentum from installations and utilization to drive growth in our joint replacement businesses."

  • "Adjusted earnings per share of $2.60" and "adjusted gross margin of 63.6%" were both pressured by the cyber incident, said (VP & CFO Preston Wells), while emphasizing, "we continue to see healthy demand across our businesses."

Outlook

  • "Despite the disruption we experienced this quarter, we are maintaining our full year guidance," said (VP & CFO Preston Wells): "organic net sales growth to be in the range of 8% to 9.5% and adjusted net earnings per share to be in the range of $14.90 to $15.10."

  • "We expect most of the first quarter's lost sales to be realized throughout the rest of the year," said (VP & CFO Wells), and outlined cadence expectations: "catch-up of revenue recognition in Q2" and impacts from "the rescheduling of certain delayed procedures" and "the fulfillment of customer orders impacted by production shutdowns" expected "into the second half of the year."

  • Versus Q4 2025’s setup, management repeated the same full-year ranges: "we expect 2026 organic net sales growth to be in the range of 8% to 9.5% and adjusted net earnings per share to be in the range of $14.90 to $15.10," said (VP & CFO Wells) in the prior quarter.

Financial Results

  • "Organic sales growth was 2.4%," said (VP & CFO Preston Wells), adding, "Pricing had a 0.3% favorable impact, and foreign currency had a 1.6% favorable impact."

  • "Adjusted earnings per share of $2.60 was down $0.24," said (VP & CFO Wells), attributing the decline to "lost manufacturing absorption related to the cyber incident" as well as "tariffs and increased interest expense."

  • "Our adjusted gross margin of 63.6% was 190 basis points lower" and "adjusted operating margin was 21.1% of sales" with "180 basis points" pressure, said (VP & CFO Wells), citing "production shutdowns due to the cyber incident" and "tariffs."

  • "Adjusted other income and expense of $97 million" rose year-over-year and the company still expects "full year 2026 adjusted other income and expense to be approximately $420 million," said (VP & CFO Wells).

  • "Our year-to-date cash from operations was $581 million," said (VP & CFO Wells), and reiterated: "For 2026, we continue to expect our full year effective tax rate to be in the range of 15% to 16%."

Q&A

  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan: asked for "more specific color" on recovery cadence and whether upside was removed; VP & CFO Wells: said recovery depends on "variability across our business," with some "caught up" later and added, "we're going to really move into those numbers" and reassess after Q2.

  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: asked about margin cadence and whether "operating margin" expectations changed; VP & CFO Wells: responded, "from a full year perspective, nothing has changed" and quarter-to-quarter cadence "might change slightly" due in part to tariffs timing.

  • Joanne Wuensch, Citi: asked about orthopaedics competition; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said, "we love our position as market leaders in robotics" and cited launches including "Mako Shoulder" mid-year and said guidance assumes "outgrow the Orthopaedic marketplace by 200 to 300 basis points."

  • Ryan Zimmerman, BTIG: asked if there was a "pause in orders" vs inability to serve; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said, "we did not see a pause in orders" but "definitely had a pause in shipments" due to being unable "to make product for almost 3 weeks."

  • Travis Steed, BofA: asked if AVS suggests more risk-taking and earlier-stage deals; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said it is "more related to the type of business than the size of our company" and "we feel very confident about" AVS.

  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: asked for Inari update; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said attrition and "Strykerize the sales force" is "mostly behind us now" and described expectations for "clear sailing" and continued innovation.

  • Samantha Kurtz, Piper Sandler: asked cyber impacts by segment; VP & CFO Wells: said orthopaedics had consigned inventory so procedures continued but "revenue recognition" lagged, while MedSurg capital items will skew recovery later; Chairman & CEO Lobo added, "Endo and Medical are more capital-intensive."

  • Elaine Cui, Raymond James: asked about Smart Hospital; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said SmartCare combines "Vocera and the care.ai businesses" and provides "seamless integration" with "Secure II beds," adding, "I'm very bullish on this for the long term."

  • Shagun Singh Chadha, RBC: asked for more granular quarterly modeling and lessons learned; VP & CFO Wells: said, "We don't guide to the quarters" and declined to split deferred procedures vs production delays; Chairman & CEO Lobo said, "the recovery of our backups, 100% of those worked" and "we're still very, very focused on recovery."

  • Michael Kratky, Leerink: asked about GLP-1 and AVS-driven cardio expansion; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said, "We're not seeing any GLP-1 impact" and added he would not predict whether future deals become "a platform deal."

  • Caitlin Cronin, Canaccord: asked Foot & Ankle and Incompass ankle; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said, "We have launched the Incompass Total Ankle" but "the PROPHECY guides have not yet been approved" and expects it to "take off" once approved.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on visibility, with repeated efforts to quantify cyber impacts and quarterly cadence, including "how much is deferred procedures versus production delays" (Shagun Singh Chadha, RBC).

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks but more guarded on granularity, highlighted by "we continue to see healthy demand" (VP & CFO Preston Wells) alongside "I will not be going into detailed sales results by business" (VP & CFO Wells).

  • Versus Q4 2025’s more upbeat framing ("Our 2025 results were outstanding" (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo)), Q1 2026 introduced a more constrained posture on disclosure ("will not enable us to provide the normal level of detail" (Chairman & CEO Lobo)).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 centered on operational disruption and recovery cadence ("The cyber incident had a big impact on our results" (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo)), while Q4 2025 emphasized broad-based momentum and multi-year margin expansion ("Our 2025 results were outstanding" (Chairman & CEO Lobo)).

  • Guidance ranges were repeated quarter to quarter, with Q1 2026 reiterating the same full-year framework amid disruption ("we are maintaining our full year guidance" (Chairman & CEO Lobo)), while Q4 2025 positioned guidance off "strong momentum entering 2026" (Chairman & CEO Lobo).

  • Analyst focus shifted from growth drivers, pricing, and capital environment in Q4 2025 to cyber-driven modeling uncertainty and recovery mechanics in Q1 2026, with multiple questions on sales and margin cadence tied to the incident.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The cyber incident occurred towards the end of the quarter" and drove "delays in revenue recognition" and "delayed shipments," said (VP & CFO Preston Wells), with recovery varying by business depending on "operating models" and "consignment structures."

  • Tariffs remained a margin headwind: Q1 results reflected tariffs and management said guidance includes "some anticipated improvements in the tariff outlook," according to (VP & CFO Wells).

  • Geopolitical exposure was cited as incremental risk: "The conflict in Iran has had a modest effect" and "persistent geopolitical risks" remain, said (VP & CFO Wells), while also pointing to long-term opportunity in the region.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as materially distorted by a late-quarter cyber incident, limiting business-level detail while keeping full-year targets intact. Executives maintained that demand, procedural volumes, and the capital order book remained healthy, and positioned recovery as a mix of Q2 revenue-recognition catch-up with a heavier second-half normalization for made-to-order and rescheduled activity. Alongside recovery execution, management highlighted the pending Amplitude Vascular Systems acquisition (expected to close in Q2), internal reporting/segment changes tied to the new Ortho Tech business, and continued product momentum led by record Q1 Mako installations and upcoming launches including Mako Shoulder on Mako 4 mid-year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NIO reports April deliveries rise 22.8% Y/Y to 29,356 units; cumulative total hits 1.11M
Saia anticipates 400-450 bps sequential operating ratio improvement in Q2 2026 as volumes accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Saia, Inc. (SAIA) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "As we moved into 2026, we remain focused on serving our customers, enhancing operational efficiency, and integrating our newer terminals into our national network" (President, CEO & Director Frederick Holzgrefe).

  • "We achieved a cargo claims ratio of 0.5%, which is our sixth straight quarter of claims ratio below 0.6%" and "service metrics continue to improve through the quarter" (President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe).

  • "Productivity continued to improve in the quarter, with touches making their strongest performance since the third quarter of 2024, improving more than 2.5% compared to the first quarter of 2025 and improving approximately 1% sequentially from the fourth quarter" (President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe).

  • "Revenue per shipment excluding fuel ramped throughout the quarter in part due to our efforts around contractual renewals, which were 6.7% for the quarter" (President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe).

  • "Revenue for the quarter was $806 million, a record for the first quarter and a 2.4% improvement over prior year" and "a shipment increase of 1% for the quarter" (President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe).

  • "We were negatively impacted in March by the 30% increase in diesel costs in a matter of a few days" and "this period of quickly rising diesel costs resulted in an approximately $3.5 million margin headwind" (Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Matthew Batteh).

  • "Revenue was a record for any first quarter, increasing by 2.4% to $806.2 million" and "our diluted earnings per share were $1.86" (Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh).

  • "We finished the quarter with $39 million of cash on hand, $12 million drawn on the revolving credit facility and $113 million in total debt outstanding" (Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh).

Outlook

  • "Q1 to Q2, typically about 250 to 300 basis points of improvement sequentially" and "this year... we think we can do about 400 to 450 basis points of improvement" (Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh).

  • "We're projecting, as we stand now, May and June to be seasonal" and "if we see May and June come together with normal seasonality, we feel like we can hit that" (Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh).

  • "The ranges that we've talked about earlier in the year, the 100 to 200 basis points is certainly within range" and "the macro is still -- diesel costs are at high levels" (President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe).

Financial results

  • "Revenue per shipment, excluding fuel surcharge, decreased 1.2% to $297.11" and "fuel surcharge revenue increased by 12.3% and was 16.5% of total revenue" (Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh).

  • "Tonnage decreased 2.1%" and "our average length of haul decreased 1.7% to 890 miles" (Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh).

  • "Salaries, wages, and benefits increased $4 million or 1%" and "head count at the end of the quarter was 6.3% lower than the first quarter of 2025" (Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh).

  • "Purchase transportation expense... increased by 7.5%" and "the increase in purchased transportation usage was driven entirely by rail" (Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh).

  • "Total operating expenses increased by 3.1%... our operating ratio increased to 91.7%" (Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh).

Q&A

  • Jordan Alliger, Goldman Sachs: "margin progression as we go Q1 to Q2"; Executive VP, CFO Batteh: "April to date, shipments are tracking up about 5.5%, tonnage up about 6.5%" and "we think we can do about 400 to 450 basis points of improvement".

  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities: asked about revenue per shipment trends and weight; Executive VP, CFO Batteh: "contractual renewals... were 6.7% for the quarter... capped by a March number that was north of 7%" and "we're... winning in these 1- and 2-day lane markets".

  • Jonathan Chappell, Evercore ISI: asked if the full-year OR improvement range shifts; President, CEO Holzgrefe: "the 100 to 200 basis points is certainly within range" and "customers think we're doing a great job... It's showing up in the April results".

  • Thomas Wadewitz, UBS: asked about weight and seasonality; Executive VP, CFO Batteh: "we saw weight per shipment increase throughout Q1" and "typically, you'd see a step up of 1% to 2%... March to April".

  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: asked when reported yield reflects renewals; Executive VP, CFO Batteh: "we'll start to see some of that in the back half of Q2"; President, CEO Holzgrefe: "we start exiting out of that... SoCal market... kind of in May".

  • Ravi Shanker, Morgan Stanley: asked end markets and tech step-changes; President, CEO Holzgrefe: "there isn't one that's necessarily outpacing another" and "I don't think that there is a -- we're quite ready to talk about any step-function changes".

  • Christian Wetherbee, Wells Fargo: asked about newer-terminal density and OR; Executive VP, CFO Batteh: "they improved margins by over 2 points on the OR side" and "they're still in the upper 90s"; President, CEO Holzgrefe: "part of the OR guide into Q2 is reflective... continued sort of leverage in the ramping new markets".

  • Stephanie Benjamin Moore, Jefferies: asked about incremental margins and free cash flow; President, CEO Holzgrefe: "we've long talked about the opportunity for Saia to operate at 80 or below OR"; Executive VP, CFO Batteh: "our plan this year was to be free cash flow positive".

  • Richa Talwar, Deutsche Bank: asked about purchased transportation and downside OR; President, CEO Holzgrefe: "we really leaned into using rail" and "as we build density... there'll be opportunities"; also, "is that out there in a tough, flattish market? I think we could achieve that".

  • Brian Ossenbeck, JPMorgan: asked about capacity in a demand inflection; President, CEO Holzgrefe: "we've got that sort of safety valve" (on PT) and "we're also going to expect that we would be compensated for that significant investment we've made".

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly positive analyst tone, centered on confirmation of demand inflection and earnings power, with repeated probing on translation of renewals into yield (e.g., "when do you think we should start to see... yields and rev per shipment numbers actually improve" — Scott Group, Wolfe Research).

  • Slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing execution and network leverage (e.g., "we remain focused" and "I'm increasingly excited about the opportunity ahead" — President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe), while staying guarded on macro uncertainty ("the ever-changing macroeconomic environment continues to create uncertainty" — President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe).

  • Versus Q4 2025, the Q1 2026 discussion sounded more oriented to improving near-term demand and sequential OR upside ("April to date... shipments... up about 5.5%" — Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh), whereas Q4 2025 carried more emphasis on the late-quarter self-insurance surprise and normalization ("unexpected adverse developments... required reserve increases... $4.7 million" — President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 highlighted diesel volatility and surcharge timing ("30% increase in diesel costs... in a few days" — President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe; "approximately $3.5 million margin headwind" — Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh) versus Q4 2025’s focus on adverse claims development ("reserve increases... approximately $4.7 million" — President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe).

  • Q1 2026 emphasized stronger pricing renewals ("6.7% for the quarter" — President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe) versus Q4 2025’s disclosed quarter average ("contractual renewals... averaging 4.9%" — President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe).

  • In Q1 2026, management called out a more optimistic sequential margin pattern ("400 to 450 basis points of improvement" — Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh) compared with Q4 2025’s discussion of typical Q4-to-Q1 deterioration ("typically a degradation of about 30 to 50 basis points" — Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh).

Risks and concerns

  • "The ever-changing macroeconomic environment continues to create uncertainty" and "diesel costs are at high levels" (President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe).

  • "Employee-related costs associated with running the business continue to be inflationary" and "claims costs continue to escalate at a rapid pace" (President, CEO & Director Holzgrefe; Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh).

  • "Revenue per shipment, excluding fuel surcharge, decreased 1.2%" with mix headwinds tied to lanes and Southern California dynamics ("Los Angeles region... shipments were still down about 14.5%" — Executive VP, CFO & Secretary Batteh).

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as operationally improving despite weather and a sharp March diesel spike, with record $806.2 million revenue and $1.86 EPS alongside higher insurance- and fuel-related cost pressure. Leadership pointed to accelerating shipments and tonnage into late March and April, stronger contractual renewals (6.7% for the quarter), and a Q2 setup that they said could deliver 400-450 basis points of sequential operating ratio improvement if seasonal demand develops normally, while reiterating that macro uncertainty and inflationary labor/claims costs remain key constraints.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump signs presidential permit for oil pipeline project partially reviving Keystone XL
data.description:
President Trump signed an order Thursday granting a cross-border ‌permit to the Bridger pipeline expansion project that would revive parts of the Keystone XL pipeline to transport Canadian oil to Guernsey, Wyoming.

The pipeline, proposed by Canadian pipeline company South Bow (SOBO) and U.S. partner Bridger Pipeline, could increase Canada's crude exports to the U.S. by more than 12% if it moves forward.

The Bridger pipeline would travel 647 miles through eastern Montana and Wyoming, along the border with North and South Dakota, and is meant to carry 550K bbl/day of Canadian crude into the U.S.

The new project would utilize a different route through the U.S. than the previous Keystone XL project, which was canceled by ​former President Biden in 2021, but will use some of the previously built pipe on the Canadian side, where Keystone XL is already fully permitted.

South Bow (SOBO) said the project is in early stages and is seeking firm commitments from ​Canadian oil shippers for 450K bbl/day on the pipeline.

Analysts have said Guernsey is not an end market for oil, so additional links would need to be built to transport oil to U.S. refining hubs.

State regulatory permits will be required, and potential court challenges remain a risk, but "provided potential shippers are comfortable with the regulatory risk, the economics of the project appear sound, and the ​pipeline will be able to offer competitive ​rates to major U.S. market ⁠hubs," Plainview Energy Analytics founder Matthew Lewis told Reuters.

data.title: Friday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
9:45 AM PMI Manufacturing Final

Based on monthly questionnaire surveys of selected companies, the Purchasing Managers' Manufacturing Index (PMI) offers an advance indication of month-to-month activity in the private sector economy by tracking changes in variables such as production, new orders, stock levels, employment, and prices across manufacturing industries.

10:00 AM ISM Manufacturing Index

The manufacturing composite index from the Institute for Supply Management is a diffusion index calculated from five of the eleven subcomponents of a monthly survey of purchasing managers at roughly 300 manufacturing firms nationwide.

Another month of moderate growth is the call for ISM manufacturing, with the index expected to be up at 53.0 in April from 52.7 in March.

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs.

data.title: Universal Display revises 2026 revenue outlook to $630M-$670M amid softer demand, while authorizing $400M buyback
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Universal Display (OLED) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "While the near-term backdrop has become more challenging, our long-term view remains unchanged," said (President, CEO & Director Steven V. Abramson), adding that "visibility across the consumer electronics value chain has become more limited in recent months" and that "we believe it is prudent to moderate our near-term revenue expectations."
  • (President, CEO & Director Abramson) highlighted shareholder returns and a new authorization: "We announced today the authorization of a new $400 million share repurchase program following the full utilization of our prior $100 million authorization."
  • (President, CEO & Director Abramson) described phosphorescent blue as a longer but continuing effort: "While this evolution is extending the development path, our conviction in the commercialization of phosphorescent blue has not wavered" and "we believe phosphorescent blue has the potential to deliver up to an initial 25% improvement in OLED panel energy efficiency."
  • (President, CEO & Director Abramson) described increased R&D tooling: "We are applying AI and machine learning at greater scale to enhance material discovery" and said these tools "allow us to predict thermal processing stability up to 10,000x faster than traditional density functional theory."
  • (President, CEO & Director Abramson) cited customer and ecosystem milestones including "new long-term agreements with Tianma and LG Display" and referenced "the industry's first commercial green PSF product targeting BT2020 specifications introduced by Visionox."
  • "Revenue for the first quarter of 2026 was $142 million" and the company revised its full-year range: "we believe it is prudent to revise our full year revenue guidance range to $630 million to $670 million from our prior guidance range of $650 million to $700 million," said (VP, CFO & Treasurer Brian Millard).

Outlook

  • "Based on current forecast, we expect second quarter revenue to be sequentially higher than the first quarter, and we continue to expect the second half of the year to be stronger than the first half," said (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard).
  • (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard) tied the reset to weaker end-demand visibility: "Near-term visibility has declined as macro pressures weigh on consumer demand assumptions, while higher memory pricing and supply constraints continue to temper end market expectations."
  • (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard) quantified the updated industry backdrop discussed in Q&A: "now there's a projection of roughly a 2% growth in square area this year," while also saying fab build plans remain intact: "the capacity plans... continue to be moving forward at full force."

Financial Results

  • (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard) reported mix-driven softness versus the year-ago quarter: "material volumes decreased by approximately 4% year-over-year" while "total revenue decreased by 14%."
  • (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard) gave segment detail: "Total material sales were $84 million" and "First quarter royalty and licensing fees were $54 million" alongside "Adesis revenue... was $4.3 million."
  • (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard) reported profitability and costs: "First quarter cost of sales was $36 million, resulting in a total gross margin of 75%" and "Operating expenses... were $63 million," with "Operating income for the quarter was $43 million."
  • (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard) cited below-the-line items: "Nonoperating expense for the quarter was $6.2 million" including "a $3 million foreign exchange loss" and "a $2.7 million investment loss."
  • (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard) reported earnings and cash generation: "Net income for the first quarter was $36 million or $0.76 per diluted share" and "We generated $109 million of operating cash flow in the first quarter."

Q&A

  • Brian Lee, Goldman Sachs: Asked about the guidance revision, area growth and smartphone weakness; (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard) said "there has been an overall change in growth expectations" with "roughly a 2% growth in square area this year," while adding premium models are "more insulated" but OLED exposure extends to mid/low-end where "memory concerns" have increased.
  • Brian Lee, Goldman Sachs: Pressed on soft China revenue and hybrid architectures; (President, CEO & Director Abramson) said "the China revenues are much lumpier" and "we believe that, that's going to pick up throughout the year," and on hybrids added: "Hybrid... means you combine a layer of phosphorescent technology with a layer of fluorescent technology" to get "efficiency from phosphorescence" plus "color points and lifetimes from fluorescence."
  • James Ricchiuti, Needham: Asked about OpEx and the blue SID paper timing plus tariff stockpiling; (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard) said OpEx growth is "trending more toward mid" and the company is "being very cautious on spend," while on blue added "It's been a few years" since UDC presented and on tariffs said stockpiling was "the largest in April" though "there certainly was some toward the end of Q1."
  • Scott Searle, ROTH: Asked about China linearity and visibility plus blue implementation; (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard) said guidance assumes a second-half ramp and "we do expect China revenues to grow in the coming quarters," while (President, CEO & Director Abramson) said multiple blue approaches "becomes even more complicated" and "it does delay -- it delays the time line," adding commercialization is "clearly a question of when, not if."
  • Martin Yang, Oppenheimer: Asked what’s driving the guidance range and new Gen 8.6 material consumption and IP protection; (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard) said capacity timing is "unchanged since February" and the change is "the overall macro environment," while (President, CEO & Director Abramson) said "We have over 7,000 patents worldwide" and "we protect them with our IP worldwide."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, centered on the guidance cut drivers and China weakness, with pointed phrasing such as China being "particularly soft" (Brian Lee, Goldman Sachs) and repeated attempts to pin down recovery visibility (Scott Searle, ROTH).
  • Neutral-to-slightly positive management tone in framing long-term demand and technology leadership, paired with more cautious near-term language, including "we believe it is prudent to moderate our near-term revenue expectations" (President, CEO & Director Abramson).
  • Versus last quarter, the prepared remarks shifted from record-year momentum to a more defensive macro framing, while still repeating long-term conviction themes around blue and multi-architecture adoption.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Full-year revenue guidance was reduced to "$630 million to $670 million" from "$650 million to $700 million" (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard).
  • The narrative shifted from Q4’s emphasis on record 2025 execution and expanding end-markets to Q1’s emphasis on reduced visibility, with Q1 citing "a more cautious demand environment, higher component costs and supply constraints" (President, CEO & Director Abramson).
  • Capital return emphasis increased in specificity in Q1 with a new "$400 million share repurchase program" (President, CEO & Director Abramson) after completing the prior authorization.
  • Analyst focus moved from blue progress metrics and competitive dynamics (Q4) toward the guidance reset mechanics, China softness, and smartphone-related demand pressure (Q1).

Risks and Concerns

  • (President, CEO & Director Abramson) cited "near-term macro uncertainty" and said "higher component costs and supply constraints are adding complexity to demand forecasting."
  • (VP, CFO & Treasurer Millard) pointed to "higher memory pricing and supply constraints" as near-term headwinds, while also stating "we continue to expect the second half of the year to be stronger than the first half" as the planning framework.
  • (President, CEO & Director Abramson) acknowledged blue timeline risk from evolving requirements: "expectations for blue are becoming more demanding and more varied" and "this evolution is extending the development path."

Final Takeaway

Management described a tougher near-term demand and forecasting environment and reduced full-year revenue guidance to $630 million to $670 million, while maintaining expectations for sequential Q2 improvement and a stronger second half. The company emphasized continued OLED capacity expansion, ongoing progress and complexity-driven timing extension for phosphorescent blue, and reiterated capital return priorities by authorizing a new $400 million share repurchase program alongside its quarterly dividend.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NewtekOne outlines 2027 EPS midpoint of $2.60 while reaffirming 2026 midpoint of $2.35
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NewtekOne (NEWT) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Really good start to 2026, EPS of $0.43... and was within our $0.37 to $0.47 guidance range," said (President, Chairman & CEO Barry R. Sloane), while adding, "We want to reconfirm our 2026 guidance of $2.35 at the midpoint and establish a $2.60 midpoint for 2027."

  • "We can announce today that we are now -- have FedNow for receiving payments for our client base," said (President, Chairman & CEO Barry R. Sloane). "We've been approved by the Federal Reserve's FedNow program and The Clearing House RTP. So we're fully approved, this is live."

  • "In the first quarter of 2026, we originated 961 loan units, up 40% year-over-year," said (President, Chairman & CEO Barry R. Sloane), adding, "In dollar terms, $391 million of loans" and that "March's momentum has continued in April with approximately 10% year-over-year growth."

  • "We ended Q1 2026 with 37,000 deposit accounts, more than doubling year-over-year," said (President, Chairman & CEO Barry R. Sloane), and described mix changes including that "consumer deposits also climbing quarter-over-quarter and year-over-year by $392 million and $668 million."

  • "Due to the exceptional deposit growth in the first quarter... leading to NIM compression," said (Executive VP & CFO Frank DeMaria), while emphasizing, "the absolute dollar balance of net interest income continues to increase" and "we saw a decline in delinquencies and NPLs, excluding government-guaranteed loans."

Outlook

  • "We've reaffirmed the EPS and origination guidance for 2026," said (Executive VP & CFO Frank DeMaria), and (President, Chairman & CEO Barry R. Sloane) said the company "establish[ed] a $2.60 midpoint for 2027."

  • "We're hoping it's a fourth quarter event, Steve, and we'd like the collateral pool to be $400 million to $500 million," said (President, Chairman & CEO Barry R. Sloane), referring to the next C&I LA securitization.

  • "I'll use the word low double-digit," said (President, Chairman & CEO Barry R. Sloane) when describing expected growth, while adding, "I think you're going to see greater diversification" and "improved credit metrics because we're going to be putting on a lot more of these C&I LA loans."

Financial results

  • "Slide 18 highlights our consolidated profitability metrics... with the first quarter return on average assets just below 2% and a return on tangible common equity approaching 15%," said (Executive VP & CFO Frank DeMaria).

  • "If you take a look at the efficiency ratio at Newtek Bank... for this particular quarter, it was 40%," said (President, Chairman & CEO Barry R. Sloane).

  • "We had a net gain on sale number for the quarter of about $26.7 million," said (Executive VP & CFO Frank DeMaria), adding it was "driven mainly by those 7(a) sales with some 504 sales sprinkled in there."

  • "We have, I think, about $383 million or $390 million of cash at the Fed," said (President, Chairman & CEO Barry R. Sloane), characterizing it as "a bit of a drag" on interest income while saying, "we had the opportunity to get deposits" and "we're going to use it."

Q&A

  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: "Does that change the trajectory of loan growth going forward?" President, Chairman & CEO Sloane: "I think the growth of loans is going to be in the bank... I'll use the word low double-digit... and I think you're going to see greater diversification."

  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: "Your LDR now super low... are you going to maintain this liquidity?" President, Chairman & CEO Sloane: "I think this is a bit excessive. I don't think we need $390 million, but... we're always going to keep a good amount of liquidity" and "We've got waiting list of people for deposits."

  • Christopher Nolan, Ladenburg Thalmann: "Why was the decrease quarter-over-quarter" in loan yields? Executive VP & CFO DeMaria: "mainly driven by the ALP loans going off balance sheet at the beginning of the quarter" and "you should see that come back to a normalized basis" in Q2.

  • Christopher Nolan, Ladenburg Thalmann: asked whether growth now had to be balanced against capital constraints. President, Chairman & CEO Sloane: "Once again, I want to be clear on this... it's really hard... to look at us quarter-over-quarter" and "no, we're not stretching," while Executive VP & CFO DeMaria added, "the leverage ratio at the holding company is 13.1%."

  • Harold Goetsch, B. Riley: asked about the 7-day loan and whether it is a competitive advantage. President, Chairman & CEO Sloane: "we don't have it broken out specifically" but pointed to "the precipitous jump" in March volume and said, "We think we continue to make more loans at double-digit rates without stretching for credit."

  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: asked about funding savings from moving C&I LA to bank deposits and potential NII trajectory. Executive VP & CFO DeMaria: "we will see a benefit from the spread" and "we are going to see a noticeable step-up as we go quarter-over-quarter," while adding, "$24 million is a little bit high."

  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: asked about SBA borrower confidence and market conditions. President, Chairman & CEO Sloane: "2025 was a challenging year for 7(a)" and said program changes made Newtek "extraordinarily competitive" versus MCA/daily debit alternatives.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to pressing around NIM/loan-yield timing, capital constraints, and liquidity levels, including Nolan saying, "Not really" when discussing income recharacterization.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive in exchanges on quarter-to-quarter optics, including (President, Chairman & CEO Barry R. Sloane) saying, "Once again, I want to be clear on this" and urging investors to view results "on a year-over-year basis."

  • Versus the prior quarter, the discussion moved from deposit-growth proof points and model validation to more detailed scrutiny of NIM compression, on-balance-sheet versus securitized income, and how rapidly deposits can be deployed into loans.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • In Q1 2026, (President, Chairman & CEO Barry R. Sloane) introduced new longer-term earnings framing by saying, "establish a $2.60 midpoint for 2027," while reiterating, "reconfirm our 2026 guidance of $2.35 at the midpoint" (Q4 2025 introduced the $2.15 to $2.55 2026 EPS range with midpoint $2.35).

  • The Q1 2026 call emphasized operational execution items not highlighted in Q4 2025, including FedNow and RTP being "live" and the company being "fully approved" for real-time payment rails.

  • Analyst focus broadened in Q1 2026 to include liquidity levels at the Fed, loan-yield timing, and capital ratio trajectory alongside growth, whereas Q4 2025 questions leaned more toward deposit-account-opening drivers, gain-on-sale trends, and SBA program disruption.

Risks and concerns

  • "One of the biggest concerns... is credit quality," said (President, Chairman & CEO Barry R. Sloane), while (Executive VP & CFO Frank DeMaria) said, "net charge-offs have picked up as the loan portfolio has seasoned," adding, "That seasoning was anticipated and captured by our CECL calculation."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Frank DeMaria) flagged near-term profitability headwinds from mix shifts: "exceptional deposit growth... leading to NIM compression," with mitigation framed as loan production strength late in Q1 that "should bode well" for Q2.

  • (President, Chairman & CEO Barry R. Sloane) cited SBA program changes affecting volumes, including "100% of the owners must be U.S. citizens" and limitations on refinancing MCA/daily debit structures, while stating Newtek’s tech-enabled underwriting created "a nice competitive advantage."

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a seasonally weaker quarter that still delivered "EPS of $0.43" within guidance, alongside record deposit-account growth and increased loan production. The call centered on deploying elevated liquidity into bank-funded lending, shifting C&I LA origination to the bank for improved funding economics, and maintaining credit stabilization trends as the portfolio seasons. Management reaffirmed 2026 guidance and provided an early 2027 EPS midpoint, while positioning real-time payments (FedNow and RTP) and technology-led underwriting as differentiators intended to support controlled, diversified balance-sheet growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: OrthoPediatrics raises 2026 revenue outlook to $263M-$267M while targeting free cash flow breakeven
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: OrthoPediatrics (KIDS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “2026 started strong with 13% first quarter revenue growth, further highlighted by significant improvements in adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow over prior year,” said (President, CEO & Director David Bailey), while noting “a softer start to the first quarter due to weather-related shutdowns in many of our OPSB clinics in January and February, but trends rebounded in March.”

  • “Importantly, we're at the earliest stages of a multiyear innovation super cycle,” (President, CEO & Director Bailey) said, citing “small contributions from recent beta launches, including 3P Hip and VerteGlide,” and adding, “Early trends are reinforcing our expectations for higher ASPs, margin expansion and improved capital efficiency as each of these products continue to scale.”

  • (President, CEO & Director Bailey) tied the strategy to 2026 targets: “we are raising our 2026 revenue guidance to a range of $263 million to $267 million… and reaffirming our expectations for approximately $25 million in adjusted EBITDA and full year free cash flow breakeven.”

  • (CFO, Principal Financial & Accounting Officer, COO & Director Fred Hite) summarized the quarter: “our first quarter of 2026 worldwide revenue of $59.4 million increased 13% compared to the first quarter of 2025,” and “Adjusted EBITDA was $2.2 million in the first quarter of 2026 compared to a loss of $0.4 million in the first quarter of 2025.”

  • (CFO, Principal Financial & Accounting Officer, COO & Director Hite) highlighted a financing change: “On March 31, we amended our existing credit agreement… to add $20 million delayed draw term loan facility,” adding, “this structure allows us to preserve liquidity and avoid dilution… interest-only through maturity in 2029.”

Outlook

  • (President, CEO & Director Bailey) stated, “we are raising our 2026 revenue guidance to a range of $263 million to $267 million in revenue, representing 11% to 13% growth and reaffirming our expectations for approximately $25 million in adjusted EBITDA and full year free cash flow breakeven.”

  • (CFO, Principal Financial & Accounting Officer, COO & Director Hite) added, “We also continue to expect to generate approximately $25 million of adjusted EBITDA, deploy approximately $10 million in [sets] and to achieve free cash flow breakeven in 2026,” and cautioned that “some periods of free cash flow will be negative and others positive.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management lifted the 2026 revenue range from $262 million-$266 million (Q4 call) to $263 million-$267 million (Q1 call), while keeping the adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow breakeven framing unchanged.

Financial Results

  • (CFO, Principal Financial & Accounting Officer, COO & Director Hite) reported segment revenue detail: “Trauma and Deformity global revenue of $43.0 million… increased 14%,” “scoliosis global revenue of $15.4 million increased 13%,” and “Sports Medicine other revenue… was $0.9 million, which stayed consistent year-over-year.”

  • (CFO, Principal Financial & Accounting Officer, COO & Director Hite) said “gross profit margin was 73%,” while “Total operating expense increased… to $51.7 million,” including “Sales and marketing… to $18.5 million” and “General and administrative… to $31.0 million.”

  • (CFO, Principal Financial & Accounting Officer, COO & Director Hite) said “GAAP net loss per share… was $0.45” and “Non-GAAP net loss per share… was $0.42,” and added liquidity and cash-use items: “We ended the first quarter with $50.9 million in cash, short-term investments and restricted cash,” “Set deployment was $2.3 million,” and “Free cash flow used… was $5.0 million.”

Q&A

  • Mathew Blackman, TD Cowen: asked about quantifying weather disruption and whether it was recaptured; (CFO Hite) replied, “typically, those appointments get rescheduled,” and said “the vast majority of those got cleared in the month of March…,” while confirming some “soot into the second quarter.”

  • Frederick Wise, Stifel: asked why guidance wasn’t moved more after strong execution; (CFO Hite) responded, “we like to wait for those things to show up and to show up in the numbers before we get too far ahead of ourselves,” and added, “we'll continue to stay conservative.”

  • [Izzy], on for Ryan Zimmerman, BTIG: asked if 22% international growth is a baseline; (President Bailey) said, “we're not going to get too far ahead of ourselves on 22% growth,” and added that EU MDR set timing is “a bit of the rate limiter, certainly not demand,” while also saying, “we do expect international to outgrow the domestic market for each quarter for the rest of the year.”

  • Joseph, on for Mike Matson, Needham: asked about operating leverage and gross margin; (CFO Hite) said, “the true leverage down to the EBITDA line is coming from G&A,” and “the leverage we saw in the first quarter will be similar in the rest of the year.”

  • David Turkaly, Citizens JMP: asked on Veraxis timing/clearance; (CFO Hite) said, “we expect first surgeries… by the end of the year,” and “it is a 510(k)… not yet cleared, but we would expect it in the back half of the year.”

  • Ravi Misra, Truist: pressed on balancing growth vs. set deployment; (President Bailey) answered, “there's certainly a healthy tension… to accelerate revenue versus generate positive free cash flow,” and said, “Our commitment is unwavering… to… free cash flow breakeven… in 2026.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with supportive framing but repeated probing on guidance conservatism, international sustainability, and whether set deployment is constraining growth (e.g., Wise: “Why leave guidance… unchanged?” and Misra: “why don't… accelerate the set deployment to capture revenue”).

  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and measured in Q&A, emphasizing demand and new-product cadence while reinforcing caution on timing (e.g., (CFO Hite): “stay conservative,” and (President Bailey): “we're not going to get too far ahead of ourselves”).

  • Versus the prior quarter, Q1’s tone leaned more execution-and-timing focused (weather, set availability, 510(k) timing), while Q4 leaned more on milestone messaging (including “our first quarter of positive free cash flow in the company's history”).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from reiteration in Q4 (“We are reiterating our 2026 revenue guidance of $262 million to $266 million”) to an increase in Q1 (“raising our 2026 revenue guidance… to… $263 million to $267 million”), while keeping “approximately $25 million” adjusted EBITDA and “free cash flow breakeven” intact.

  • Product cycle framing remained consistent (both calls used “multiyear… super cycle”), but Q1 added more near-term commercialization detail, including (President Bailey) on VerteGlide: “This success is triggering our move to full market release… in the second quarter.”

  • Analyst focus rotated from Q4’s shareholder-value optionality and new adjacencies to Q1’s operational pacing questions (set availability, ramp timing, and conservative guidance posture).

Risks and Concerns

  • (President Bailey) flagged operational disruption risk from weather impacts on clinics, describing “weather-related shutdowns in many of our OPSB clinics in January and February.”

  • (President Bailey) and (CFO Hite) repeatedly described timing constraints around inventory and approvals, including Europe where set timing was described as “the rate limiter,” and Veraxis where clearance timing was called “a bit of a wildcard.”

  • (CFO Hite) indicated free cash flow will fluctuate intra-year: “some periods of free cash flow will be negative and others positive,” even as the company targets full-year breakeven.

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as a rebound quarter after early weather disruption, driven by international strength and continued 20%+ OPSB growth, while positioning 3P Hip and VerteGlide as early contributors within a “multiyear innovation super cycle.” The company raised its 2026 revenue range to $263 million-$267 million and reaffirmed approximately $25 million of adjusted EBITDA and full-year free cash flow breakeven, while emphasizing that set availability, EU MDR rollout logistics, and 510(k) timing will influence how quickly new products translate into reported results.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: ConocoPhillips outlines $12B-$12.5B 2026 capex range while staying on track for $7B free cash flow inflection by 2029
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ConocoPhillips (COP) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We generated $2.4 billion of free cash flow and returned $2 billion of capital to our shareholders," said (Chairman & CEO Ryan Lance), while framing the quarter against Middle East-driven market disruption and emphasizing that "we will continue delivering base dividend growth competitive with the top quartile of the S&P 500" and "remain committed to returning 45% of our CFO."
  • "In Alaska, we're winding down another successful winter construction season with the Willow project now 50% complete," said (Chairman & CEO Ryan Lance), adding that ConocoPhillips "recently completed our 4-well exploration program in Alaska" and "Port Arthur LNG project continues to progress very well with first LNG expected next year."
  • "We generated $1.89 per share in adjusted earnings and $5.4 billion of CFO," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO Andrew O'Brien), adding "Capital expenditures were $2.9 billion" and that the company "returned $2 billion to our shareholders during the first quarter, $1 billion in ordinary dividends and $1 billion of share repurchases."
  • "For production, the midpoint of our annual guidance is updated at 2,310,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO Andrew O'Brien), while stressing, "To be clear, this is not a call on when we think the conflict will resolve. We're simply trying to provide a clear and transparent framework for you to model."

Outlook

  • "The midpoint of our second quarter production guidance is 2,200,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien), attributing the change to "the full exclusion of Qatar production from guidance for the quarter, the Surmont royalty rate adjustment and planned second quarter maintenance."
  • "For capital spending, we're updating our guidance to a range of $12 billion to $12.5 billion versus our prior guidance of about $12 billion," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien), citing "slightly more Permian activity over the second half of the year" and added uncertainty tied to "timing for NFE and NFS spending."
  • "Full year guidance of $10.2 billion is unchanged," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien), while stating it reflects "a $400 million reduction from 2025" and that management "remain[s] confident in realizing the full $1 billion run rate by year-end."

Financial Results

  • "We produced 2,309,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO Andrew O'Brien), noting it "includes the impacts of the Middle East conflict on Qatar volumes and higher royalty rates at Surmont from higher oil prices" and adding that Lower 48 output was "1,453,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day."
  • "We ended the quarter with cash and short-term investments of $6.7 billion as well as $1.2 billion in liquid long-term investments," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien).
  • "We remain unhedged on oil and LNG to ensure we capture the price upside," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien), adding that "shareholders are directly participating in this upside as we remain committed to returning 45% of our CFO."

Q&A

  • Scott Hanold, RBC Capital Markets: asked for COP’s view of physical vs. financial oil market dynamics and likely operator reactions; (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said "for about 2 months now, we've had about 10 million barrels a day of production offline" and that the shortfall is being absorbed by "refinery run cuts and demand curtailments," while (Chairman & CEO Lance) said "the floor probably is going to have to raise up" relative to the prior mid-cycle view.
  • Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs: asked for a Willow mark-to-market and key milestones; (Executive Vice President of Global Operations & Technical Functions Kirk Johnson) said Willow is "50% complete," the team completed "the entirety of the gravel scope," and "this is on track" for "early oil expectation ... for 2029."
  • Wei Jiang, Barclays: asked why COP leaned into Permian activity and what would drive further flex; (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said the $250 million midpoint CapEx increase is about maintaining "steady state" and responding to partner ballots, while (Executive Vice President of Lower 48 & Global HSE Nicholas Olds) said "we're adding another Permian rig" and "we're not going to elect out of low cost of supply" nonoperated wells.
  • Douglas Leggate, Wolfe Research: questioned sticking with the 45% CFO payout amid higher prices and buybacks; (Chairman & CEO Lance) said the company buys back shares "on a dollar cost average" and "we do want to make sure we hit the 45% made up between the base dividend and whatever shares we're repurchasing."
  • Francis Lloyd Byrne, Jefferies: asked why OpEx guidance wasn’t lowered after a strong Q1; (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said, "it is only the first quarter" and management wants "a little bit more time" before changing guidance.
  • Devin McDermott, Morgan Stanley: asked about Equatorial Guinea (EG) tolling and Port Arthur marketing; (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said COP has "already placed the first 5 million tons" of Port Arthur Phase 1 and discussions are "intensifying," while (Executive Vice President Johnson) said the EG tolling agreement "pushes the life of that asset ... well into the 2030s."
  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: asked how Middle East disruptions affect LNG macro and Qatar project timing; (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said Qatar disruption equates to "200 cargoes" not delivered and COP sees a "structural change" toward shortages, while (Executive Vice President Johnson) said QE expects NFE/NFS delays "to the tune of months."
  • Joshua Silverstein, UBS: asked about divestitures and Port Arthur Phase 1 stake; (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said the $5 billion program has "$3 billion ... already behind us" and COP is "not going to be schedule driven," adding, "we certainly don't need to sell anything" at Port Arthur while it is under construction.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing around macro volatility, capital returns, and timing risk, including questions on whether to change payouts and how to model conflict impacts.
  • Management tone was neutral to slightly positive, repeatedly emphasizing framework and durability, including "To be clear, this is not a call on when we think the conflict will resolve" (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) and "these are no-brainers" on Permian continuity (Chairman & CEO Lance).
  • Compared with Q4 2025, the current call’s tone was more conflict-driven and uncertainty-focused, while Q4 emphasized execution and multi-year free cash flow trajectory without the same level of geopolitical disruption discussion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 updated 2026 capital guidance to "$12 billion to $12.5 billion" (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) versus the prior quarter’s "2026 capital spend guidance of about $12 billion" (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien).
  • Q1 guidance language added explicit conflict modeling, including Qatar being "excluded from second quarter production guidance" (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien), while Q4 guidance centered on cost and capital reductions and "modest growth for the year" (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien).
  • Analyst focus shifted from organic vs. M&A posture, reserves, and breakeven trajectory in Q4 to near-term operational and financial implications of Middle East supply disruptions, Qatar LNG timing, and short-cycle U.S. activity response in Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The macro environment remains volatile and pretty impossible to predict," said (Chairman & CEO Ryan Lance), while (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said guidance updates reflect "the uncertainty surrounding the Middle East conflict" and included a range around NFE/NFS spending timing.
  • (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) flagged that the supply shortfall is being absorbed by "refinery run cuts and demand curtailments" and said COP is "downgrading our view of global oil demand to be flat year-over-year" if the conflict persists.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a quarter with $2.4 billion of free cash flow and $2 billion returned to shareholders, while updating 2026 guidance to reflect Middle East uncertainty, including Qatar’s exclusion from Q2 production guidance and a higher capital range of $12 billion to $12.5 billion tied to modest Permian additions and LNG project timing variability; leadership reiterated its 45% CFO return objective, highlighted Willow at 50% complete with early oil expected in 2029, and pointed to LNG positioning in Equatorial Guinea and Port Arthur as central to its longer-term free cash flow growth plan.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Imax outlines 2026 targets including $1.4B global box office and 160-175 system installations
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: IMAX Corporation (IMAX) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "With the heart of our formidable slate now rolling out, we remain very confident in our 2026 guidance, including a record $1.4 billion in global box office this year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "Project Hail Mary... has now earned more than $90 million in IMAX, more than double our initial projections." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "IMAX global box office in the current quarter-to-date is over $100 million, up over 10% year-over-year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "Year-to-date, we signed agreements for over 40 new and upgraded IMAX systems worldwide... most recently with our biggest deal ever in one of the most productive markets in the world, our 10-system agreement with HOYTS in Australia and New Zealand." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "I'm happy to share that I'm making excellent progress, and I'm gradually resuming oversight of the business and involved in all key strategic decisions." (CEO & Director Richard L. Gelfond)

Outlook

  • "We remain very well positioned to achieve our 2026 guidance, including record global box office of $1.4 billion, 160 to 175 system installations worldwide and adjusted EBITDA margin... with at least 45%." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "We remain confident in our forecast of total adjusted EBITDA margin of more than 50% in the coming year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "[T]here's always a mix between the regions of box office... and the amount that we're investing into marketing in this year... And so that's where the margins can ebb and flow." (Executive VP & CFO Fernandes)

Financial results

  • "First quarter adjusted net income grew 33% to $10 million, while adjusted EPS grew $0.17, up $0.04 year-over-year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "IMAX delivered revenues of $81.4 million, a decline of $5 million year-over-year, driven by decreased revenues in Greater China." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "Adjusted EBITDA declined in line with revenue, down $6 million year-over-year to $31 million... adjusted EBITDA margin was 38% compared to 43% in the prior year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "In our Content Solutions segment, revenues declined 8% to $31 million... [and] China box office declined 62% due to an exceptionally strong Q1 in the prior year and the timing of major releases this year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "IMAX cash flow from operations was $4 million compared to $7 million in the prior year and includes $8 million in higher year-over-year lease incentives provided to exhibitors to support the building of new IMAX auditoriums." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "As of March 31, we held $146 million in cash and $300 million in debt with a net leverage of 0.86x." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)

Q&A

  • Andrew Crum, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "[D]oes that assume a global box office of $1.4 billion?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "[Y]es... there's always a mix between the regions of box office... and the amount that we're investing into marketing in this year... this is just from a guidance perspective, providing that guidance with respect to the floor of the 45%."
  • Andrew Crum, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "[A]ny disruptions created by the U.S.-Iran conflict...?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "No, not at all... we have about 35 locations within the region... majority are continuing to operate."
  • Michael Hickey, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: "[F]rame... the growth opportunity network-wise in Australia and Japan?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "Australia is one of the strongest performing regions... Some of the locations have TSAs up to $4 million... We're only about 13% penetrated... Japan is only 47% penetrated."
  • Eric Handler, ROTH Capital Partners, LLC, Research Division: "$8 million of higher lease incentives, is that part of that strategy?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "It is... Investing the $8 million to help grow the network faster... all within the respect of making sure that we hit our return hurdles."
  • Chad Beynon, Macquarie Research: "[W]hat gives you the confidence that China will come through in '26?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "[W]e're managing it on an annual global portfolio... there's a lot of Hollywood titles that have strong potential in China... [and] the local language slate is stacking up as well."
  • David Karnovsky, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: "[A] read-through... to kind of your relationship with the studio?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "[W]e feel it's a pure marketing play... Marvel fans, we believe that they're among the savviest... moviegoers out there."
  • Steven Frankel, Rosenblatt Securities Inc., Research Division: "Where are we in that process today?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "[W]e're only about 28% to 30% penetrated in India... it does take long to install... Ramayana Part 1... [is] a really big deal because over 90% of their box office is local language."

Sentiment analysis

  • "[A]djusted EBITDA margin guidance with a floor of 45%" and China/local-language pacing were recurring analyst focus areas, with questions framed as clarification-seeking rather than confrontational. (Analyst sentiment: neutral)
  • "We remain very confident in our 2026 guidance" and "There has never been a better time to be in the IMAX business" anchored prepared remarks, while Q&A included more qualifying language such as "margin will fluctuate" and "it's a mix" when discussing profitability cadence and China timing. (Management sentiment: slightly positive)
  • In Q4 2025, management used more record-year emphasis ("record-breaking year," "We beat projections across almost every key financial metrics"), while Q1 2026 messaging leaned more to reaffirmation of full-year targets and explaining geographic mix and marketing spend. (Tone shift vs. prior quarter: from celebratory to execution-focused)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 leadership presence shifted, with "Natasha will handle today's call and Q&A session" and CEO Richard L. Gelfond noting he is on a "Leave of Absence" and "gradually resuming oversight," versus Rich Gelfond leading prepared remarks and Q&A heavily in Q4 2025.
  • The 2026 framework was reiterated in Q1 2026 ("$1.4 billion" global box office; "160 to 175" installations; EBITDA margin floor "45%"), consistent with Q4 2025 guidance language ("projected $1.4 billion"; "160 to 175"; "floor of 45%").
  • China discussion in Q1 2026 emphasized diversification and timing normalization ("more evenly spread throughout the year") versus Q4 2025 framing that Q1 would be the lowest quarter and China would be more balanced due to title timing.

Risks and concerns

  • "[W]e faced a significant comp against last year's massive Ne Zha 2" and "China box office declined 62%" were cited as near-term pressures, with mitigation framed as timing and mix: "We expect IMAX box office in China to be more evenly spread throughout the year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • Marketing spend and margin variability were highlighted as factors affecting EBITDA cadence: "we are marketing titles well in advance" and "margin will fluctuate normally quarter-by-quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Fernandes)
  • On geopolitics, management said: "No, not at all" regarding U.S.-Iran conflict impact on annual outlook, while noting the Middle East footprint and continued operations. (Executive VP & CFO Fernandes)

Final takeaway

IMAX management reiterated full-year 2026 targets (including $1.4 billion global box office, 160 to 175 system installations, and an adjusted EBITDA margin floor of 45%) while attributing Q1 year-over-year pressure primarily to Greater China comps and timing. Management emphasized early Q2 box office momentum, a broadened and higher-visibility slate across Hollywood and local language, and continued network expansion—highlighted by a 10-system HOYTS agreement—alongside disciplined operating expenses and selective capital deployment (including lease incentives) aimed at accelerating new IMAX locations ahead of major releases.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
